texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We focus on a behavioral model, that has been recently proposed in the literature, whose rational can be traced back to the Half-Full/Half-Empty glass metaphor. More precisely, we generalize the Half-Full/Half-Empty approach to the context of positive and negative lotteries and give financial and behavioral interpretations of the Half-Full/Half-Empty parameters. We develop a portfolio selection model based on the Half-Full/Half-Empty strategy, resulting in a nonconvex optimization problem, which, nonetheless, is proven to be equivalent to an alternative Mixed-Integer Linear Programming formulation. By means of the ensuing empirical analysis, based on three real-world datasets, the Half-Full/Half-Empty model is shown to be very versatile by appropriately varying its parameters, and to provide portfolios displaying promising performances in terms of risk and profitability, compared with Prospect Theory, risk minimization approaches and Equally-Weighted portfolios.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theoretical models of individual behavior in decision-making under risk conditions begun to have a solid foundation in the mid-1900s with the development of the Expected Utility Theory (EUT) by von Neumann (von Neumann et al., 1944). According to EUT, if the preferences of individuals in risky decision making satisfy some fundamental axioms, the Decision Maker (DM) maximizes the expected utility function, and such choice is considered to be rational (Keeney et al., 1993): in this framework, individuals are taken to be rational agents. However, many criticisms have followed over the years due to the discrepancy between the theorized rational behavior and the real one, which can actually be influenced by multiple factors.
texts.text: Theoretical models of individual behavior in decision-making under risk conditions begun to have a solid foundation in the mid-1900s with the development of the Expected Utility Theory (EUT) by von Neumann (von Neumann et al., 1944). According to EUT, if the preferences of individuals in risky decision making satisfy some fundamental axioms, the Decision Maker (DM) maximizes the expected utility function, and such choice is considered to be rational (Keeney et al., 1993): in this framework, individuals are taken to be rational agents. However, many criticisms have followed over the years due to the discrepancy between the theorized rational behavior and the real one, which can actually be influenced by multiple factors.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The evolution of the literature on decision-making under risk has long been driven by the discovery of paradoxes (Fox and Poldrack, 2014; Luce et al., 2008; Wu et al., 2004). Old paradoxes such as the Allais one (Allais, 1953) and its variations (Kahneman and Tversky, 1979) challenged the descriptive power of EUT. For this reason, over the last 60 years, decision theorists have been trying to develop a theory of choice that can give a satisfactory description of decision-makers' behavior in risky situations.

texts.orig: Although several theories have been proposed over time (see, e.g., Fox and Poldrack, 2014; Starmer, 2000; Wu et al., 2004), Prospect Theory (PT) (Kahneman and Tversky, 1979) and Cumulative Prospect Theory (CPT) (Tversky and Kahneman, 1992) have emerged as the leading descriptive paradigm for modeling decision-making under risk (Camerer, 1998; Fox and Poldrack, 2014; Starmer, 2000; Wu et al., 2004) and have inspired a large body of theoretical and empirical work (Abdellaoui, 2000, 2002; Camerer, 1992; Diecidue and Wakker, 2001; Gonzalez and Wu, 1999; Karni and Safra, 1987; Luce, 2014, 2001; Machina, 1982; Prelec, 1998; Quiggin, 2012; Schmeidler, 1989; Starmer and Sugden, 1989; Tversky and Wakker, 1995; Viscusi, 1989; Wakker, 1994, 1996, 2001; Wakker et al., 1994; Wu and Gonzalez, 1996, 1998, 1999; Yaari, 1987). Despite this apparent success, several scholars have questioned the underlying assumptions and applicability of PT and CPT, which seem not to describe some empirical evidence (Baltussen et al., 2006; Blavatskyy, 2005; Brandstätter et al., 2006; Hertwig et al., 2004; Humphrey, 1995; Levy and Levy, 2002; Lopes and Oden, 1999; Marley and Luce, 2005; Neilson and Stowe, 2002; Payne, 2005; Starmer, 2000; Starmer and Sugden, 1993; Weber and Kirsner, 1997; Wu and Gonzalez, 1999; Wu and Markle, 2008; Wu et al., 2005).

Despite this apparent success, several scholars have questioned the underlying assumptions and applicability of PT and CPT, which seem not to describe some empirical evidence (Baltussen et al., 2006; Blavatskyy, 2005; Brandstätter et al., 2006; Hertwig et al., 2004; Humphrey, 1995; Levy and Levy, 2002; Lopes and Oden, 1999; Marley and Luce, 2005; Neilson and Stowe, 2002; Payne, 2005; Starmer, 2000; Starmer and Sugden, 1993; Weber and Kirsner, 1997; Wu and Gonzalez, 1999; Wu and Markle, 2008; Wu et al., 2005).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PT and CPT are, thus, at the same time, 'one of the most confirmed and falsified decision-making models currently in circulation' (Wakker, 2010). However, Birnbaum (2008) has recently produced systematic empirical cases that confute both PT and CPT approaches and have received considerable attention in the relevant literature (Fox and Poldrack, 2014; Luce et al., 2008; Wakker, 2010). In fact, the author has presented 11 new paradoxes showing 'self-contradiction or systematic false predictions' (Birnbaum, 2008, p. 463), leading to the conclusion that PT and CPT cannot 'be retained as a descriptive model of decision making' (Birnbaum, 2008, p. 464).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The transfer of attention exchange (TAX) model 'correctly predicts results with all 11 new paradoxes' (Birnbaum and Stegner, 1979; Birnbaum and Chavez, 1997; Birnbaum, 2008, p. 463). But, while this model fits the data better than PT and CPT (Birnbaum, 2008; Fox and Poldrack, 2014; Wakker, 2010), it is less parsimonious (Wu et al., 2004, p. 408), involving the presence of a large number of free parameters, and does not have 'the particularly tractable form of prospect theory, with psychological interpretations for its parameters' (Wakker, 2010, p. 351).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Cenci et al. (2015), a simple decision-making under risk approach for positive lotteries has been proposed to explain the well-known 'old and new' paradoxes at a stroke. The rationale behind the resulting model can be easily understood through the metaphor of the 'glass half-full, glass half-empty': different individuals may evaluate the same situation differently and are typically divided between those who focus on positive aspects (the optimists/glass half-full) and those who focus on negative ones (the pessimists/glass half-empty). The Half-Full/Half-Empty (HF/HE) model suggests that a DM's evaluation of a choice situation may depend on how the DM assesses the difference between outcomes above and below the mean value of the lottery considered. The DM's evaluation of the lottery (and the consequent attitude towards risk) depends on two parameters only: the DM's degree of optimism/pessimism, and a probability distortion parameter that determines the DM's decision weights. In Corradini (2022), the author investigates how to suitably calibrate this model to explain 'old and new' paradoxes, even in the case of negative lotteries (Note that, in Corradini (2022), only calibration of negative lotteries in Kahneman and Tversky (1979) is considered since negative lotteries in Birnbaum (2008) are trivially explained).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Compared to other decision-making models that have been recently proposed to address the paradoxes in Birnbaum (2008), the advantages of the HF/HE approach are twofold. On the one hand, it is very intuitive, as it is closely related to the Expected Value (EV) criterion, and the related parameters have a clear and well-established behavioral interpretation (see, e.g., Hurwicz, 1951; Arrow and Hurwicz, 1972; Kahneman and Tversky, 1979; Tversky and Wakker, 1995). On the other hand, the HF/HE approach is highly parsimonious and flexible, providing an extremely simple way of modeling behavior, based on just three parameters.

On the other hand, the HF/HE approach is highly parsimonious and flexible, providing an extremely simple way of modeling behavior, based on just three parameters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Behavioral models for portfolio selection incorporate investor's perception of risk into the decisionmaking process, avoiding the pitfalls of suboptimal investment choices. Theoretical evidence exists in the literature on risk reduction in portfolio selection models based on the behavior of DMs who are naturally loss averse (Hens and Bachmann, 2008; Grishina et al., 2017). Benartzi and Thaler (1995) focus their study on a particular loss aversion concept, i.e. the so-called myopic loss aversion, which is key in PT, to explain the equity premium puzzle (see also Mehra and Prescott, 1985) for DMs who evaluate their portfolios annually. Levy and Levy (2003), among the Pareto-optimal mean-variance portfolios, propose to select the ones with the highest prospect utility, in case of normally distributed asset returns. Pirvu and Schulze (2012) generalize the results of Levy and Levy (2003) for elliptic symmetric distributions of risky assets. De Giorgi et al. (2007) apply the PT approach to portfolio selection, assuming equally likely historical scenarios for the asset returns. The PT portfolios are obtained by solving a nonconvex optimization problem through a heuristic. Other scholars investigate portfolio selection models based on CPT (see Bernard and Ghossoub, 2010; He and Zhou, 2011; Consigli et al., 2019). Barro et al. (2020) compare different portfolio selection models, including models based on PT and CPT, and approaches based on risk minimization, where risk is measured by variance and mean absolute deviation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we refine the approach in Cenci et al. (2015) and Corradini (2022) by tailoring it to portfolio selection problems. More precisely, we generalize the HF/HE approach to the context of mixed (i.e. positive and negative) lotteries and discuss financial and behavioral interpretation of the HF/HE-related parameters. We develop a portfolio selection model based on the HF/HE approach that results in a nonconvex optimization problem, for which we suggest an alternative, equivalent MixedInteger Linear Programming (MILP) formulation. By means of the empirical analysis we give, based on three real-world data sets, the HF/HE model is shown to be very versatile by appropriately setting its parameters, providing portfolios that display promising performances in terms of risk and profitability, compared with Prospect Theory and risk minimization approaches and the Equally-Weighted portfolio.
texts.text: In this paper, we refine the approach in Cenci et al. (2015) and Corradini (2022) by tailoring it to portfolio selection problems. More precisely, we generalize the HF/HE approach to the context of mixed (i.e. positive and negative) lotteries and discuss financial and behavioral interpretation of the HF/HE-related parameters. We develop a portfolio selection model based on the HF/HE approach that results in a nonconvex optimization problem, for which we suggest an alternative, equivalent MixedInteger Linear Programming (MILP) formulation. By means of the empirical analysis we give, based on three real-world data sets, the HF/HE model is shown to be very versatile by appropriately setting its parameters, providing portfolios that display promising performances in terms of risk and profitability, compared with Prospect Theory and risk minimization approaches and the Equally-Weighted portfolio.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we describe how to apply the HF/HE approach to portfolio selection. More in detail, in Section 2.1, we introduce some preliminary concepts related to the decision-making model under risk in Cenci et al. (2015) and Corradini (2022). In Section 2.2, we extend the HF/HE approach to the context of positive and negative lotteries and present a detailed discussion about the financial interpretation of HF/HE parameters. In Section 2.3, we adapt the HF/HE approach for portfolio selection purposes, obtaining a nonconvex optimization problem, for which, in Section 2.4, we propose a (MILP) equivalent formulation. In Section 3, we present the PT-based portfolio selection model and two minimum-risk portfolio strategies that referred to for comparative purposes. In Section 4, we provide an extensive empirical analysis based on three real-world datasets, where the performance of the HF/HE portfolio is compared with that of Prospect Theory, Equally-Weighted, minimum variance and Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) portfolios. Conclusions are drawn in Section 5.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3 The HF/HE portfolio selection model
texts.text: 2.3 The HF/HE portfolio selection model

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we adapt the HF/HE approach to portfolio selection problems. We consider asset returns defined on a discrete probability space with T states of nature, where each state has a probability of occurrence equal to π t , t = 1 , . . . , T . Furthermore, we use a look-back approach where the realizations of the discrete random returns correspond to the historical scenarios, which are equally likely, i.e., π t = 1 T ∀ t , as typically assumed in portfolio optimization (see, e.g., Mansini et al., 2007; Carleo et al., 2017; Bellini et al., 2021, and references therein). If p kt is the price of asset k at time t , the return of asset k at time t is denoted by r kt = p kt -p k ( t -1) p k ( t -1) . Assuming an investment universe composed of n assets and denoting by x the vector of portfolio weights, the portfolio return at time t is given by R t ( x ) = ∑ n k =1 x k r kt . For all models we consider, feasible portfolios are those identified by the budget constraint ∑ n k =1 x k = 1 and the no-short-selling requirements x k ≥ 0 k = 1 , . . . , n .
texts.text: In this section, we adapt the HF/HE approach to portfolio selection problems. We consider asset returns defined on a discrete probability space with T states of nature, where each state has a probability of occurrence equal to π t , t = 1 , . . . , T . Furthermore, we use a look-back approach where the realizations of the discrete random returns correspond to the historical scenarios, which are equally likely, i.e., π t = 1 T ∀ t , as typically assumed in portfolio optimization (see, e.g., Mansini et al., 2007; Carleo et al., 2017; Bellini et al., 2021, and references therein). If p kt is the price of asset k at time t , the return of asset k at time t is denoted by r kt = p kt -p k ( t -1) p k ( t -1) . Assuming an investment universe composed of n assets and denoting by x the vector of portfolio weights, the portfolio return at time t is given by R t ( x ) = ∑ n k =1 x k r kt . For all models we consider, feasible portfolios are those identified by the budget constraint ∑ n k =1 x k = 1 and the no-short-selling requirements x k ≥ 0 k = 1 , . . . , n .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Other portfolio selection models to be compared with
texts.text: 3 Other portfolio selection models to be compared with

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We introduce several portfolio selection models to be compared with the HF/HE approach. More precisely, in Section 3.1, we briefly describe the behavioral portfolio selection model based on PT. In Section 3.2, we present two minimum risk approaches, using variance and MAD, as a risk measures, respectively. Furthermore, we also test the so-called Equally Weighted (EW) portfolio, defined by x k = 1 n , k = 1 , · · · , n , whose performances would seem to be barely improved by optimized portfolios in practice (DeMiguel et al., 2009).
texts.text: We introduce several portfolio selection models to be compared with the HF/HE approach. More precisely, in Section 3.1, we briefly describe the behavioral portfolio selection model based on PT. In Section 3.2, we present two minimum risk approaches, using variance and MAD, as a risk measures, respectively. Furthermore, we also test the so-called Equally Weighted (EW) portfolio, defined by x k = 1 n , k = 1 , · · · , n , whose performances would seem to be barely improved by optimized portfolios in practice (DeMiguel et al., 2009).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1 Prospect Theory portfolio
texts.text: 3.1 Prospect Theory portfolio

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Kahneman and Tversky (1979) suggest to interpret individuals' experimentally observed economic choices by modeling their satisfaction level through a nonconcave function. A DM exhibits a riskaverse attitude toward gains and a risk-seeking attitude toward losses. A crucial property of PT is loss aversion: individuals are more sensitive to losses than gains (see Sun, 2009). Furthermore, among different alternatives, investors evaluate potential gains and losses with respect to a reference point. They overestimate the probabilities of significant gains and losses. Accordingly, on the one hand, the satisfaction levels of a DM are modeled by a function that is concave for gains and convex for losses, thus incorporating a risk-averse attitude for gains and a risk-seeking attitude for losses. The value function is steeper below the reference since people tend to focus more on losses than gains. On the other hand, a DM transforms objective probabilities through a weighting function, by underestimating medium-high probabilities and overestimating low probabilities associated with extreme events (see Section 2.2.2).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Conclusions
texts.text: 5 Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Taking a cue from Cenci et al. (2015) and Corradini (2022), we have extended the so-called HF/HE behavioral approach to encompass the general case of mixed positive and negative lotteries. Also, we have been able to present a novel and detailed discussion about the financial meaning of the model's relevant parameters. Such analysis, in turn, prompted us to tailor this strategy to portfolio selection purposes: as a result, we devised a nonconvex optimization model together with its MILP reformulation. Concerning testing and validation, we have presented an extensive empirical analysis based on several real-world datasets, which confirms, by means of numerical evidences, the out-ofsample competitiveness of the method.
texts.text: Taking a cue from Cenci et al. (2015) and Corradini (2022), we have extended the so-called HF/HE behavioral approach to encompass the general case of mixed positive and negative lotteries. Also, we have been able to present a novel and detailed discussion about the financial meaning of the model's relevant parameters. Such analysis, in turn, prompted us to tailor this strategy to portfolio selection purposes: as a result, we devised a nonconvex optimization model together with its MILP reformulation. Concerning testing and validation, we have presented an extensive empirical analysis based on several real-world datasets, which confirms, by means of numerical evidences, the out-ofsample competitiveness of the method.
